name: Online Coop
description: Players cooperate in a shared online session where actions, state, progression, and failures must remain consistent across clients.
design_notes: Online co-op needs explicit authority for every shared action. Movement may be predicted, inventory may be transactional, objectives may be server-owned, and cosmetics may be client-owned, but those boundaries must be documented so reconnects, rollback, host migration, and late joins do not create divergent worlds.
design_purpose: Let separated players share progression, combat, exploration, crafting, or survival decisions.
Create social reliability around help, rescue, shared resources, and coordinated objectives.
Support long sessions where disconnects and latency are expected rather than exceptional.
player_fantasy: Players feel present in the same world and trust that teamwork, rewards, and failures are shared fairly.

parameters.name: session_id
parameters.description: Authoritative online session identifier used by players, saves, invites, and reconnects.

parameters.name: connection_id
parameters.description: Network connection handle mapped to a player_id while the client remains connected.

parameters.name: authority_owner_id
parameters.description: Authority that accepts final gameplay changes for shared state.

parameters.name: replicated_state_channel
parameters.description: Named channel used to route updates by reliability, priority, and ownership rules.

parameters.name: prediction_window_ms
parameters.description: Client prediction horizon used before authoritative correction is expected.

parameters.name: reconnect_token
parameters.description: Token used to restore player_id, inventory locks, objectives, and position after disconnect.

edge_cases: A client reconnects after their character died, moved zones, or held a locked item transaction.
The host leaves while shared objectives, crafting queues, or base edits are mid-commit.
Predicted movement reaches a pickup first but the server grants it to another player.
A late joiner receives world state while enemies, projectiles, and objective events are changing.
common_bugs: Client-owned inventory or crafting changes are accepted without server transaction ordering.
Movement corrections restore position but not ability cooldowns, animation state, or carried objects.
Session saves serialize local client state rather than the authority snapshot and lose other players' progress.
Reliable channels are overused and chat, inventory, or objective traffic blocks movement updates.
balancing_notes: Shared rewards reduce friction, while per-player rewards reduce loot conflict; both need clear UI language.
Drop-in support improves accessibility and social play, but objective scaling and enemy spawning must handle changing party size.
Client prediction should prioritize feel without letting local clients authoritatively claim scarce rewards.
accessibility_notes: Support text chat, pings, subtitles, and non-voice coordination for players who cannot or prefer not to use voice.
Make disconnect, reconnect, and migration states visible so players understand whether progress is safe.

implementation_notes.unity: Use a clear ownership table for NetworkObjects and gameplay services: characters can predict movement, but inventories, objectives, rewards, and saves should commit through server RPCs with transaction ids. Store reconnect_token mappings to player_id and replay authority snapshots before accepting new input.
implementation_notes.godot: Use MultiplayerAPI RPCs or a custom server to separate client requests from authority mutations. Track connection_id, player_id, and replicated_state_channel names explicitly, and send late join snapshots before enabling interactive input.
implementation_notes.unreal: GameMode and server-owned Actors should approve shared state, while PlayerController and Character handle prediction and correction. Use replicated properties for persistent state, RPCs for requests, and seamless travel or reconnect metadata to restore party members safely.
implementation_notes.web: For WebSocket sessions, keep the authoritative simulation on the server and send delta snapshots with server_tick. Clients may predict local movement but must reconcile inventory, damage, objective, and reward results from server events.

relationships.reason: Online co-op usually needs a lobby or session flow for party formation, readiness, and reconnect state.
Revive mechanics add cooperative recovery pressure and reasons to protect teammates.
Authoritative online co-op is structurally hard to combine with player-controlled rewinds without rollback architecture.

example_games.title: Left 4 Dead
example_games.note: Uses online cooperative play with shared objectives and teammate recovery.

example_games.title: Deep Rock Galactic
example_games.note: Uses networked co-op for class teamwork, objectives, and shared extraction.

example_games.title: Portal 2
example_games.note: Uses an online co-op campaign with shared puzzle state and partner coordination.
